data.title: Shake Shack: Hitting The Buy Zone Again With Strong Momentum In LA
data.description:
Honestly, I figured Shake Shack (NYSE:SHAK)—Danny Meyer’s New York fast-casual darling—would just be trading sideways by now after a tepid quarter.

But a couple of things popped up that made me revisit the thesis before my usual Earnings Preview:

  • It seems that Shake Shack's stock fell along with many other fast-casual names after the Q2 earnings announcement. Most of them are still feeling the squeeze—on one hand, fast-food chains are back with their $5 promos, and on the other, casual dining is giving guests more bang for their buck than pretty much any other segment right now. To see this, just look at how same-store sales at chains like Texas Roadhouse (TXRH) and LongHorn Steakhouse (DRI) are holding steady. Even Applebee's (DIN), once an underdog, is seeing a resurgence in same-store sales as it runs its 'Really Big Meal Deal' and '2 For $25,' taking a break from meaningless LTOs. This basically dropped Shake Shack right into my 'buy' zone, which makes flipping the rating here a smart move to catch the bottom again (just like we pulled off back in April).

  • Shake Shack’s CFO, Katherine Fogerty, spoke at the Goldman Sachs (GS) 32nd Annual Global Retailing Conference 2025, and her comments gave us some pretty interesting hints about what’s ahead.

So let’s break it down, one topic at a time.

No More Gaps, Please!

Funny enough, let me kick this off with a little provocation. Do you remember what really drove Shake Shack to post its weakest same-store sales in more than three years back in Q1 FY 2025—the same quarter where we nailed the bottom?

In fact, it wasn't just one reason, but two:

  1. Bad weather in several parts of the country (including NY, where most of Shake Shack's restaurants are concentrated), with February '25 being considered the 'worst month for restaurants' since January '24.
  2. Shake Shack ended up running the Black Truffle LTO for a whopping seven months—way longer than planned (September '24 to April '25). While they were holding onto that one item, fast-food and fast-casual competitors kept cranking out new launches like clockwork. That made Shack’s higher price point look a lot less attractive, and traffic slid -4.6% year-over-year.

That gap in LTOs during Q1 was a wake-up call. Management realized they couldn't afford to let the calendar go quiet like that again. Kind of like what happened with Kura Sushi (KRUS), where a big gap in IP caused same-store sales to drop 5.3% year-over-year.

Now it seems things have changed.

As I mentioned in my last write-up, in Q2 they launched some very interesting LTOs like the Dubai Chocolate Pistachio Shake (launched at the end of April and quickly went viral), the Summer BBQ Menu (launched on May 2nd, with four burger options and two special fries), and the Summer Shake Menu (also launched in May, with three summer-themed shakes).

And, voilà! Same-store sales grew 1.8% year-over-year!

Of course, we're not talking about strong traffic growth.

After all, Shake Shack is facing increasingly difficult comparisons, and the promotional environment is heating up, but it's a huge difference.

Heading into Q3, a few Q2 LTOs carried over, but thankfully no repeat of the Black Truffle misstep—they rolled out fresh themed LTOs instead. The first thing that stands out is that both the Dubai Chocolate Pistachio Shake and the Summer BBQ Menu are still running nationwide.

The company has started rolling out the Jalapeño Ranch Menu in Malaysia, where they have three locations (July 2), with two burger options (Jalapeño Ranch Burger and Jalapeño Ranch Chicken), Jalapeño Ranch Chicken Bites, and Jalapeño Ranch Fries.

But in the United States, they are focusing on the French Onion Soup Menu. Basically, it's a menu of three burgers (French Onion Soup Burger, French Onion 'Shroom Burger, and French Onion Shack Stack) and two sides (Parmesan Garlic Fries and Beer-Battered Onion Rings).

Interestingly, Katherine mentioned that these promos have really boosted traffic at Shake Shacks outside of New York and D.C.

For perspective, it was mentioned that some markets like Denver (seven locations, including a non-traditional one at Denver International Airport) and Los Angeles (about a dozen locations, the second-largest market for Shake Shack behind only New York) have seen high single-digit year-over-year same-store sales growth!

Meanwhile, markets like Dallas, Houston, Miami, and Orlando are also holding steady.

New York is definitely feeling the squeeze, which is a bit worrying. But since most of the expansion is happening outside the city, we can probably chalk this up as a temporary hiccup.

How about we try to cross-reference this with granular data?

Honestly, seeing Shake Shack pull high single-digit growth in L.A. really stood out to me. It’s a clear sign they’re actually grabbing market share in that region.

Doubt? Let's take a look at the traffic for QSRs there.

Geolocation-based traffic data indicates that California QSR traffic fell 2.6% year-over-year in July, followed by another similar decline in August. In fact, in almost every month of 2025 (excluding January), grocery stores outperformed fast-food/fast-casual restaurants there.

This was to be expected after AB 1228. But Shake Shack is gaining market share there? I really didn't expect that.

To give you an idea, only In-N-Out saw year-over-year growth among the largest chains there. Is this something like a resurgence of fast-casual restaurants in California? Too early to say, but it's interesting.

Colorado had a similar story. Traffic fell 1.7% year-over-year in July and then another 2.7% in August. Shake Shack also gained market share there.

Regarding New York, we can see from the same data that traffic fell 1.9% in July and an even further 4.1% in August, making things truly difficult there. Burger King (QSR), for example, saw a whopping 10% year-over-year traffic drop in August, while other chains like McDonald's (MCD) are also feeling the pinch (-4.4% in August). So, it's safe to say that Shake Shack must have reported a mid-single-digit year-over-year traffic drop there.

But does that really move the needle? Honestly, not by much. LA seems to be partly balancing out New York's softness, and with traffic holding up in other secondary markets, overall things still look fine.

What's interesting is that if you zoom out and take a long view, Shake Shack is slowly but surely becoming a spot the average American is becoming increasingly aware of and enjoying.

Looking at the popularity tracker, for example, you can see that in mid-2021, about 60% of Americans knew the brand. Of these, ~35% liked it and ~5% disliked it. Today, almost 80% of Americans know it, with popularity reaching 45.5% (100 bp higher) and almost 6% disliking it.

Taking a Look at Restaurant-Level Economies

Something few analysts (and even management) talk about is restaurant-level profitability data. And, let's face it, it's at the heart of a successful expansion project.

When you think of Shake Shack's growth story, you immediately think of their TAM of 1,500 locations nationwide. Now, that's no easy task.

If they really want to 'democratize luxury,' as they love to say, they've got to get creative: bundle promotions, drive-thru deals, and everything in between. I even wrote an article focusing solely on the operational side of this.

But today, let's talk about the financial side.

Something incredible that I only learned when I heard the CFO speak is that they reduced construction costs by approximately 10%, increasing the ROI for each Shack opened. But by how much?

Let's understand how things stand today:

  • Each Legacy location cost approximately $3.6 million each. Since they reduced construction costs by 10%, we're now targeting $3.24 million per Shack.
  • Though not mentioned, Lean Shacks cost $3.3 million. Assuming they also pulled that 10% construction cost reduction out of the hat, we'd be looking at something closer to $2.97 million.
  • Each Shack is delivering AWS of $78,000, which gives a projected AUV of ~$4 million (+1.3% year-over-year).
  • Restaurant-level margin of 23.9% (in Q2 FY 2024 it was 22%).

Just take a look at this:

So, as you can see, Shake Shack is achieving a one-year payback period for each prototype model.

A great position to scale.

But remember, beef prices are eating into the margins of many restaurant chains and will likely reduce Shake Shack's restaurant-level margins to ~22% (a drop of almost 8% in restaurant-level EBITDA).

Precisely for this reason, management is actively seeking to maintain a portfolio of suppliers to achieve better prices. The goal is to expand restaurant-level margins by at least 50 bp every three years—and they've already exceeded that recently.

However, the approach remains simple: reduce turnover (both in the kitchen and among general managers) and optimize the supply chain. And we'll see how they address this in H2 FY 2025.

Trying to Catch the Bottom One More Time

I said it before: at $100, Shake Shack was back in 'buy' territory. And even now, I still see the stock trading like a high-growth restaurant play in the medium term. It's the classic situation of accumulating at the bottoms that Mr. Market gives you.

That said, with my price target sitting at $125–$130, we're looking at a clean 25–30% upside from today's levels. That's a way better setup than the modest 5.8–8.3% upside I laid out in the last piece.

Remember, so far, the EBITDA guidance of $210–220 million remains intact, and I'm basing it on a scenario:

  • Minimum dilution;
  • Self-financing growth (no increases in long-term debt);
  • EV/EBITDA multiples in line with the historical average (with the market pricing it as a growth story).

With the CFO’s traffic update on the table, I don’t see much need for a full earnings preview this time—unless we get some kind of curveball. My call is for low-to-mid single-digit same-store sales growth year-over-year, with traffic holding steady.

data.title: ASGN Incorporated: Downgrading To 'Sell' On Negative Growth And Bearish Technicals
data.description:
Intro

Our last commentary on ASGN Incorporated (NYSE:ASGN) was in June of last year, when we rated the IT-oriented company a 'Hold.' Decreasing profitability, an overextended valuation, and concerning technicals were chief reasons why we saw limited upside in the stock at the time. As we see below on the 20-year chart, shares have lost close to 37% over the past 26 months, which is a sizable opportunity cost for long-term shareholders, given the S&P has rallied 44%+ over the same timeframe. Furthermore, although many of ASGN's long-term technical indicators, due to the sustained patterns of lower lows in recent times, are oversold, there is every possibility that ASGN has entered a bear market due to the breaking of the multi-year trendline, which commenced in 2009. Furthermore, the rising ADX trend-following indicator demonstrates that the downturn continues to gain traction, all things remaining equal. Here are some fundamental reasons that back up our bearish thesis at this time.

Fundamentals Not Lining Up With Financials

In the company's most recent quarterly report (announced 7/23/2025), management reported both top- and bottom-line beats (revenues of $1.02 billion and normalized earnings per share of $1.17). It should be noted, however, that GAAP earnings ($0.67 per share or net income of $29.3 million) fell significantly short of the comparable quarter of the previous year ($47.2 million). Earnings revisions have improved over the past three months (6 upward revisions versus 1 downward revision), but as mentioned, present results remain well behind what we are accustomed to in this company, with margins also beginning to suffer as a result.

To put it simply, management's most recent comments regarding the forward-looking fundamentals of the company were too general in nature for our liking and not specific to ASGN. Furthermore, the trends that the CEO laid out didn't address any near-term catalysts, despite the long-term tailwinds ASGN is expected to benefit from.

ASGN Relying On Broad Industry Trends

Management's pitch to investors rests on the following broad assumptions, where

  1. Demand (customer spending habits) for AI-oriented commercial consulting solutions is expected to overcome near-term macroeconomic uncertainties. TopBloc is expected to continue its growth path in this segment, as ASGN believes its customers will have no other choice but to step up their AI-oriented spending, meaning costs will have to be cut in other areas of their businesses.
  2. The muted level of contract awards in the federal business will only be temporarily adversely affected by how the US administration has rolled out DOGE. Furthermore, ASGN management believes DOGE's close alignment with its core solutions and the fact that the one big beautiful bill should only accelerate the digital transformation from legacy systems is bullish for ASGN going forward.
  3. ISG believes there will be a sizable increase in both public and private IT labs as IT system modernization remains crucial in this ever-accelerating information age. ASGN management (due to how the company is set up, where a custom-made approach can be offered to varying types of agencies alike) believes its products and services will automatically rise in demand as a result towards the latter end of this decade.

As alluded to earlier, however, ASGN's net income margin on a trailing basis comes in at a muted 3.5%, well down from the company's 5-year average of 5.91%. Therefore, given the company's margin erosion in recent times, you feel the above 'assumptions' have to take place for ASGN to increase its returns over time (constituting growing its top-line revenues at a much faster clip than the negative growth rates that are presently being reported).

Forward-Looking Growth Projection

Therefore, given ASGN's concerning long-term technicals, negative earnings growth, and below-average GAAP margins, it can be a good exercise for long-term investors to estimate the projected annualized rate of return over, let's say, something like a 5-year period. Therefore, with the stock trading at $50.84 as I write, and with trailing GAAP earnings coming in at $3.18 per share, we can state that the company's trailing price-to-earnings ratio comes in at 15.99. Despite recent growth rates, bulls may actually find value here, given the company's 5-year P/E average comes in at a higher 20.95.

Now, given that ASGN does not pay a dividend, let's say we want to earn a 12% annualized return from this stock over the next five years. This means ASGN stock will be trading at approximately $89.60 after 5 years. Furthermore, with the stock's earnings trading at a slight discount, let's assume that the price of ASGN's earnings will return to their mean at the end of the 5 years (20.95 trailing GAAP multiple).

What this means is that in September 2030 (5 years from now), the company's earnings based on our desired projections will have grown to $4.28 per share on a trailing 12-month basis. This means ASGN's earnings will have to grow by just over 6% per year to give us our desired income (a finishing share price of $89.60).

However, given how negative earnings growth continues in ASGN, we find it difficult to believe at this juncture how the company will achieve 6% bottom-line annualized returns at least over the near term. We do acknowledge that ASGN is expected to grow its earnings by strong double-digit percentages in fiscal 2026 and fiscal 2027, but a sustained run of bearish EPS revisions could easily impair these projections at an accelerated clip over time. Furthermore, from a valuation perspective, although earnings, as mentioned, are currently available at a discount, ASGN's rising goodwill ($2.14 billion) presents challenges, especially if acquisition-led growth does not pan out as originally envisaged.

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, we rate ASGN a sell at present due to the long-term death cross & corresponding trendline break, negative EPS growth trends, declining margins, and rising goodwill. The company's long-term debt of $1.21 billion is also substantial, where rising debt will do nothing to reset investor sentiment over time. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Athene Holding's 7.0% Yield Has A Margin Of Safety
data.description:
Inflation moderated off its peak in 2022, but it has begun to rise again in recent months, primarily due to the impact of tariffs on product prices. In addition, the Fed recently shifted to a dovish policy, stating that it will prioritize a reduction in unemployment and just keep an eye on inflation. Lower interest rates are likely to fuel economic growth and reduce the unemployment rate but they are also likely to lead inflation higher, as there is a trade-off between unemployment and inflation.

As inflation has proved much more persistent than initially anticipated and is likely to increase even further, income-oriented investors should pursue high yields but without compromising safety. The 7.25% junior subordinated debentures (bonds) of Athene Holding Ltd. CAL NT 64 (NYSE:ATHS) are currently trading nearly 4% above their value of $25, and hence they are offering a 7.0% yield. Given the promising business prospects of Athene Holding (more on this below), the 7.0% yield has a wide margin of safety.

Overview Of The Bond

The prospectus of the bond offers all the necessary details that investors should know. The bond matures on 3/30/2064 and offers a 7.25% coupon, with a par value of $25. The bond becomes callable on 3/30/2029 at its par value. On that date, its annual interest will be recalculated based on the formula: United States 5-Year Bond Yield (US5Y) plus 2.986%. If the 5-year U.S. Treasury Yield is at its current level of 3.63% in 2029, the bond will yield 6.62% at its par value. After 2029, the yield of the bond will be recalculated every 5 years based on this formula. When the bond was issued last year, it was rated Baa2 by Moody's and BBB- by S&P.

Business Overview

Athene Holding is a global leader in retirement services, with more than $400 billion of total assets and presence in the U.S., Bermuda, Canada, and Japan. The primary goal of Athene Holding is to provide financial security to retirees by offering them a wide variety of retirement income and savings financial products. The company also provides solutions to corporations. Athene Holding merged with Apollo in 2022 but it maintains its own capital structure and credit profile.

Athene Holding enjoys positive business momentum. In the most recent quarter, it achieved $21 billion of organic inflows, the second-best performance in its history. Even better, the company seems to have ample room for future growth, given the $14 trillion total addressable market for retirement in the U.S. and more than 80 million U.S. citizens expected to become retirees over the next 25 years.

During the second quarter, 71% of total sales were generated by financial institutions. This channel requires strong ratings as well as high-quality operations and technology. As a result, this channel is characterized by high barriers to entry and hence Athene Holding has a meaningful business moat. This moat is clearly reflected in the dominant business position of Athene Holding, which is confirmed by a 12% market share in the fixed annuity market. In the first half of this year, the company generated $2 billion of sales at each of Wells Fargo, JPMorgan and LPL Financial.

As shown in the chart below, Athene Holding has grown its organic inflows at a stellar pace over the last nine years.

The company is not only the market leader in retail annuities; it is also the market leader in funding agreements and has the #2 position in cumulative market share in pension group annuities since it entered this business in 2017.

Moreover, Athene Holding has a strong balance sheet.

It has an A+ credit rating from S&P and Fitch and an A1 rating from Moody's. It also has $71 billion of available liquidity and $3.1 billion of excess capital. As its interest expense consumes just 13% of its operating income, it is evident that the company is in a markedly strong financial position. The strong balance sheet has resulted primarily from the accumulation of earnings, which have grown at a stellar pace. To be sure, Athene Holding has more than quadrupled its net income over the last decade. Given also the aforementioned gigantic size of the total addressable market, the company is likely to remain on its growth trajectory for many more years.

Upside Potential - Downside Risk

As mentioned in the introduction, the Fed recently succumbed to the continued pressure of the U.S. government and decided to prioritize a decrease in unemployment vs. a decrease in inflation. To this end, the Fed just cut interest rates by 25 basis points and provided a dot plot that includes additional interest rate cuts this and next year.

Lower interest rates provide a strong tailwind to fixed-income securities, such as the bond of Athene Holding. If the expected interest rate cuts materialize, the bond of Athene Holding is likely to appreciate. On the other hand, given the risk of the bond being called from 2029, the bond is unlikely to appreciate much from its current price of $25.94. Overall, if interest rates decrease further, the bond may rally 2%-4%, up to $26.50-$27.00, but not higher due to the risk of a redemption.

It is also important to note that the future path of interest rates is highly uncertain due to the risk of a resurgence of inflation. Lower interest rates are likely to reinvigorate economic growth but at the same time, they are also likely to lead inflation to higher levels. Due to the impact of tariffs on the price of imported goods, inflation has climbed from a 4-year low of 2.3% in April to 2.9% lately. If inflation rises much further, the Fed may have to change its policy and raise interest rates again at some point in the future. In such a case, the bond of Athene Holding is likely to come under pressure.

A resurgence of inflation above 3.0% will also render the 7.0% yield-on-cost of the bond less attractive, as it will reduce the real (after inflation) yield significantly. To cut a long story short, the near-term outlook is favorable for the bond thanks to the expected interest rate cuts, but there is high uncertainty regarding the future path of interest rates.

Final Thoughts

When a bond offers a 7.0% yield, it sometimes signals the existence of some risk related to the issuer of the bond. However, this is not the case for the bond of Athene Holding. The company has been growing its earnings consistently and has ample room for future growth. The only material risk for the 7.0% yield of the bond of Athene Holding is the adverse scenario of a resurgence of inflation, which may lead the Fed to raise interest rates in the future. In such a case, the bondholders will still be receiving a 7.0% yield-on-cost, but that yield will be less attractive due to higher inflation and higher yields available elsewhere in the market.

data.title: Expect Interest Rates To Plunge As We Barrel Toward A Recession
data.description:
On Sept. 17, the Federal Reserve decided, for the first time in nine months, to cut interest rates. The central bank decided to cut rates by 25 basis points, which in many respects is a positive thing. Long term, interest rates that are lower than what we have are believed to be beneficial for the economy. This is because they make it easier to borrow and to use the debt taken on to grow.

Unfortunately, while I am still bullish about the economy in the long run, I don't necessarily associate their decision to cut rates this time with a bullish outlook for the market in the near term. And I don’t think I’m alone. Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell described the cut as a "risk management cut." Significant risks seemed to be building up in the labor market. And as anybody who knows a lot about the Federal Reserve understands, keeping prices under control is only one part of what the institution does. The other one of its two mandates is to maintain maximum employment. And with broader economic data looking rather depressed, I still believe that the economy is heading toward a stagflationary recession.

Interest rates… Long-term and short-term

Conceptually, as I stated already, the decision by the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates was a positive thing. Lower interest rates help to grease the wheels of the economy. And I can almost guarantee that further interest rate cuts are on the horizon. After all, as you can tell by looking at the chart above, while interest rates are nowhere near all-time highs, they can certainly move lower than where they are now. In fact, for much of the last 20 years, they have averaged lower than where they are now.

In the image above, you can see the famous "dot plot" that the Federal Reserve puts out a few times a year. This illustrates where each member of the bank believes interest rates should be right now. The midpoint of these 19 dots helps determine where interest rates ultimately end up. And what we see for 2025 is a midpoint reading of between 3.50% and 3.75%. The current rate cut brings us down to between 4% and 4.25%. This means that the central bank is currently anticipating another cut of 25 basis points before the year is out.

In subsequent years, we should see even more cuts. Keep in mind that most economists believe that interest rates should be, long term, somewhere between 2% and 3.5%. For next year, the midpoint is currently 3.25% to 3.50%. So this means that the bank is currently anticipating two rate cuts next year. Absent inflation coming in significantly greater than what even I anticipate, I think that there is a high probability that rates will fall much faster than this. I would be shocked if we are in the 3% to 3.25% range at the end of 2027, as the Federal Reserve currently expects. And that's because of my overall view of the health of the U.S. economy.

Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell described this interest rate cut as a "risk management cut." He specified that while the bank expects inflation to continue growing through the rest of this year and into next year, those price increases are driven by tariffs. But so far, the effects have not been terribly large. The bigger risk, it seems, relates to jobs data. He specifically mentioned that "the labor market is softening, and we don't need it to soften anymore [and] don't want it to."

Expect a recession

I will not sit here and tell you that I am the ultimate authority on all things related to the economy. While I have a degree in economics, which is one of two college degrees that I have, the other being a Master's in Accountancy, I acknowledge that the experts at the Federal Reserve, as well as at other financial institutions, certainly are more astute and in tune with the economy than I am. Having said that, I do find the revisions that the Federal Reserve has made to economic projections to be curious, to say the least.

In the image above, you can see expectations for real GDP, which is inflation-adjusted GDP, for this year all the way through 2028 and beyond. If you look closely at the image, you can see how, for this year, real GDP is expected to be somewhere between 1.4% and 1.7%. That represents an upward shift compared to the 1.4% to 1.5% that the institution expected in June of this year. Next year, real GDP is expected to be stronger as well, coming in at between 1.8% and 1.9%. The previous expected range was closer to the 1.6% to 1.7% range, with greater variability in potential outcomes in the 1.4% to 2.1% range.

Keeping in mind that the experts at the Federal Reserve are more knowledgeable than I am, I feel confident in disagreeing with their assessment of things. I expect tariffs to put a meaningful drag on the economy over the next year or so. And as I have been saying, including in an article that I published in August of this year, a recession is almost certain to be around the corner. This is not just my opinion, and it has nothing to do with political bias. Rather, it comes down to a fundamental understanding of how the economy works and is backed by hard data.

For instance, jobs data is starting to become increasingly worrying. For June of this year, the U.S. economy lost 13,000 jobs. That represented a downward revision from the 14,000 jobs gained reported for the same month previously. While there was a decent upward revision in jobs for July to 79,000 compared to the 73,000 previously reported, trailing three-month job data is looking worse and worse.

*Numbers in Thousands. Represents Month-to-Month Jobs Changes

If we use the ending point of June of this year, we have seen a downward revision to 55,000 jobs on a trailing-three-month basis compared to the 64,000 previously reported. For July, the downward revision was from 35,000 to 28,000. Furthermore, for August of this year, the U.S. economy added just 22,000 jobs. If we ignore the data from May and June of this year, this would be the weakest month that the U.S. economy has seen from a job creation perspective since the 183,000 jobs that were lost in December 2020.

*Numbers in Thousands. Represents Trailing 3 Months Jobs Changes.

Non-farm job openings data is also looking abysmal. For July of this year, the U.S. economy saw 7.18 million job openings. That is the lowest point, if we ignore the 7.10 million reported in September of last year, since the 6.77 million job openings that were seen in December 2020 when the economy was finally rebounding from the COVID-19 pandemic. In fact, job openings data is getting pretty close to pre-pandemic levels. While that might seem like a positive thing, since it might mean that employers are getting more control over bargaining power relative to employees, which could itself help to push inflation down, we need to keep in mind that the population of the United States is quite a bit larger now than it was before the pandemic. In 2019, the population was about 328.3 million. By 2024, it had grown 11.8 million to hit 340.1 million. Over the long haul, job openings should be increasing, not decreasing.

Another issue here is that the unemployment rate is rising. For August of this year, the unemployment rate hit a multi-year high of 4.3%. That is still low compared to most historical data, but it is the highest point that we have seen since the 4.5% that we registered in October 2021. To see job openings decline and the unemployment rate increase in concert with one another is disconcerting to say the least. It signals a broader weakening in the employment picture.

Consumer confidence is also problematic. As the chart above illustrates, consumer confidence data has experienced an uptick recently. But it is still near multiyear lows. Inflationary pressures and a worsening job market will likely keep consumer confidence levels depressed for quite some time. And, historically speaking, declines in consumer confidence have typically precipitated recessions. This can be seen by looking at the grey columns in the aforementioned chart. Those represent windows of time during which an official recession was occurring.

Long term, there could be some benefits from some weakening. As the image above illustrates, the 30-year fixed mortgage rate has recently dropped. As of Sept. 18 of this year, it was registered at 6.26%. That's down from the 6.58% that we saw one month earlier. In theory, lower mortgage rates should bring with them more home-buying activity. And at some point, especially as interest rates fall further, that absolutely will take place. Unfortunately, I suspect that we will still have to deal with a lot of pain for quite some time. As the chart below illustrates, home prices have been dropping for quite a while now. After surging because of supply chain issues and quantitative easing that all occurred around the time of the COVID-19 pandemic or shortly after, affordability issues and high interest rates that exacerbated affordability issues ended up causing a decline in home buying activity. But even leading indicators are proving to be a challenge for the industry. For July of this year, for instance, home completions were down 8.4% year-over-year. Housing starts were down 6%. And residential building permits, which are the leading indicator for what the construction picture might look like moving forward, happened to be down 11.1%.

It is my professional opinion that tariffs are the primary culprit here. Keep in mind that the Federal Reserve was on a path to cut interest rates at a much faster pace before tariffs were implemented. But these are a tax on the American consumer since companies overwhelmingly passed the costs on to the purchasers of their goods. Foreign countries do not eat the tariffs. And while some companies abroad might make concessions, especially with major suppliers, the overwhelming majority of the burden will fall on the American consumer. This weakens the economic outlook for the economy while simultaneously pushing prices higher. As I have stated already, this is a recipe for stagflation, which is a situation where prices rise even as the job market weakens.

Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell mentioned that companies have been largely absorbing increased prices up to this point. But he also warned that the picture is pointing to a change here where firms are starting to push those prices onto consumers. I know that anecdotal data doesn't really mean anything. But almost every single company that I analyze that deals in physical goods has mentioned hiking prices. And if you look at my track record of publications on Seeking Alpha, you will see that I might be the most active, or certainly one of the most active, contributors on this platform. So I have seen many companies come forward with admissions of price hikes.

There is data to back up the view that prices will rise. We need only look at the data from the Producer Price Index (or PPI) as an example. In August of this year, the year-over-year rise in prices was 2.1%. Other than in January of this year, when we saw a jump to 2.4%, this is the highest point that we have on record after February of 2023. If we strip out food and energy from the equation, especially since energy prices have been on the decline, the 2.9% reading that we get is the highest we have since the 3.3% that was reported in April of 2023. Food prices have also been rising.

After experiencing a surge following the conclusion of the COVID-19 pandemic, food prices began to drop around the middle of 2023. But then, starting in November of last year, prices began to rise materially. There are certain aspects of the food industry that are unrelated to tariffs. Examples would be the role that disease has played in pushing up egg prices and the climate change effects that have negatively impacted beef production. But the United States does import a lot of its goods, including fruits, vegetables, coffee, cocoa, and even beef. Plus, many of the items that are used in the preparation and packaging of food are also affected by tariffs. Now, food prices are climbing. For last year, PPI data for food showed anything from a 3% drop year-over-year to a 2.9% increase through September of 2024. But then, in November, prices began rising. And since this year began, food prices have been up anywhere from 2.9% to 5.9% on a year-over-year basis. Data like this does not bode well for broader economic activity and points to even more weakness in consumer confidence moving forward.

Takeaway

In my professional opinion, market participants should be prepared for a stagflationary recession in the coming months. Absent some major change in policy, I think it is all but guaranteed that prices will rise as broader economic conditions weaken. Although the stock market has currently bucked the trend, notching all-time highs, and even though interest rate cuts will ultimately benefit things in the long run, I fully expect a reckoning to be around the corner. So, while I am bullish about individual companies, I have personally, as I detailed in my previous article in August of this year about my expectations of a recession, been more defensive in how I structure my portfolios.

data.title: Applied Materials: Valuation Upside Exists, But I'm Awaiting Clarity On China
data.description:
Applied Materials, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMAT) is a semiconductor manufacturing equipment manufacturer for the semiconductor industry, such as deposition and etching machines and metrology systems. The issue with AMAT is that they don't specifically dominate one part of the manufacturing supply chain like ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) with EUV lithography, Lam Research Corporation (LRCX) with their deposition Altus systems and KLA Corporation (KLAC) with their wafer inspection machines.

With the 4 having a huge bounce back in the last month, with the semiconductor manufacturing-related stocks rising, is it now AMAT's time or will they underperform again compared to their peers?

With soft guidance still lingering for investors, the next earnings will further signal to investors whether AMAT is dead money as an AI stock going red YoY or a rebound is in place with AMAT catching up to the advanced levels of peers' equipment. Hence, I'm rating AMAT at a 'Hold'.

Thesis

The AI boom still has shown no signs of slowing down, with Oracle recently reporting a 359% increase in RPO in Q1 FY26 (remaining performance obligation) when their prior quarter only guided for a 100% increase in all of FY26.

AMAT is positioned for growth with 3D DRAM and NAND, but its most recent guidance has shocked investors with soft guidance, which could signal a slowdown in AI. I argue that this is a short-term shock for AMAT, as more and more fabs are expected to be built, especially with Intel's bailout by the US government with a 10% stake. With Intel having more cash to invest into advanced 14A fabs, this could benefit AMAT as more machines and systems will be needed and it is simply good news if none of AMAT's customers are struggling. With AMAT winning Intel's EPIC Supplier Award for 2025, the good news for Intel should also reflect on AMAT. The problem is that AMAT is positioned and has market share in legacy nodes, and as the supply chain gets more and more advanced, AMAT gets left behind.

Many manufacturing stocks have already rebounded, such as ONTO, KLAC, ASML and Lam Research. AMAT on the other hand, has been relatively flat compared to these names as the stock has underperformed the S&P500 across a 6-month scale, 1-year scale and year-to-date scale. With valuations still at attractive prices, AMAT with any positive sentiment, could rebound alongside its close peers as the stock is filled with negative sentiment, such as its recent guidance. I argue for a stock that has so little investor confidence, as seen from the poor stock price performance, that any time of bullish news could easily lift the stock up to higher premiums like its relative peers.

AMAT's Business

AMAT does a range of systems and machines, such as PVD (Physical Vapor Deposition), CMP (Chemical Mechanical Planarization), Thin Film Deposition, Etch, ALD (Atomic Layer Deposition) and metrology/inspection.

Most of the definitions can be seen above from AMAT's glossary, and AMAT is most dominant in PVD, CMP and some Thin Film Deposition. The others, like etching, are dominated by other AI giants like Lam and Tokyo Electron so AMAT doesn't have a dominant market share, as Lam has the most advanced etch tool available, their product family Akara.

AMAT's Future

Despite a double beat in the most recent quarter, investors never really cared for present results but rather cared for the future due to recent macroeconomic uncertainty. Guidance was set rather low, with 81% of S&P500 companies beating both top and bottom lines, with one of the most notable segments being Information Technology. Their FY25 Q3 guidance was significantly disappointing, with falling sales and falling net incomes, as seen with below consensus EPS and revenue. I argue the only positive sentiment remaining is their margins, with non-GAAP gross margin guided at 48.1%, while the previous year reported a gross margin of 47.5%, showing that despite sales falling, the cost of goods sold remains the same. With the prior years' operating margin at 29.3% for FY24 Q4, anything above that number and I would argue, would still be solid numbers with AMAT struggling with sales.

Buying AMAT is sort of a bet on 2 aspects: whether macroeconomic pressure with the US and China eases with AMAT having a majority revenue segment in China, or they can find external customers outside of China, which may be difficult with the most recent numbers.

AMAT's China exposure increased YoY from 32% of its total net revenue to 35%, with the revenue growing 18.3% YoY, which is larger than its total revenue growth in FY25 Q3 of 7.73%.

From their recent earnings call:

For Applied's business, there are 3 main factors that mute our outlook for the quarter ahead. First is digestion of capacity in China. Second is our large backlog of pending export license applications, where we have taken a conservative position and assumed none of these licenses will be issued in the next quarter. And third is nonlinear demand from leading-edge customers, which is primarily linked to market concentration and fab timing.

China is a huge concern for AMAT, as its largest revenue segment is expected to be heavily and negatively affected. With other major segments like the US, Europe, Japan and Korea having revenues falling YoY or being relatively flat/not a growth driver, the affected China revenue is not a good sign for AMAT.

Recent news doesn't aid AMAT's case either; SK Hynix and Samsung had their authorizations to obtain US semiconductor equipment for operations in China revoked, which further affects AMAT, as China is such a large segment for them. With SK Hynix having an estimated 30%-40% of DRAM and NAND output from China, AMAT stands to lose out on DRAM revenue, which has also been falling as a % of their total revenue, as the number could further fall. DRAM has only grown its revenue by 1% YoY with the percentages above, which could be further affected by this licensing change. With Morgan Stanley expecting DRAM to be the 2026 growth driver for wafer fab equipment, AMAT losing out on this market should be a huge negative sentiment for investors, as AMAT is missing out on a catalyst, as noted that KLAC is expected to outperform the wafer fab equipment growth with DRAM.

AMAT has undisputed market leadership in PVD, but with such constant restrictions on the US and China, it creates an opportunity for the Chinese firms to close the gap despite AMAT being the market leader. From 2019 to 2024, it was estimated that China's market share in deposition increased from 2% to 7%. Although AMAT is the leader and perhaps has the most advanced PVD machines, they are under threat and have very weak/no pricing power for legacy nodes. Analysts from Mizuho downgraded AMAT recently, stating:

AMAT is exposed to PVD/Sputtering, Plasma CVD, and 28nm+ Conductor Etch (~60% of Revs for AMAT, but only 40% of Revs for LAM), losing share,” Mizuho said, adding that Chinese rivals such as Naura and AMEC are “strategically gaining share in PVD, CVD, and etch (>28nm).

With more and more competition from China and the gap starting to close, a majority of their revenue could be taken by these Chinese firms, especially for legacy nodes where demand is lower and they have weak/no pricing power. With China's revenue being its largest revenue segment, as well as the Chinese government actively mentioning avoiding buying from US firms like Nvidia, this will lead to AMAT losing market share. As a Chinese tech firm, it is more rational to buy from other Chinese firms, especially at legacy nodes, as if AMAT wants to compete with these Chinese firms, margins would have to take a hit due to higher costs (having to ship to China, Chinese firms just make it locally, etc). It is also simply more rational with SK Hynix and Samsung's authorization getting revoked, as Chinese firms could easily risk the fact that their machine supplier can't supply them anymore due to uncontrollable factors or simply go with a Chinese firm which won't have that risk. AMAT doesn't have the benefit and leisure like ASML, where they entirely control the EUV lithography machine supply and have technology with gaps lasting potentially decades to catch up, so AMAT is at risk of losing its China revenue with high competition and the gap closing down.

With earnings in just 2 months, positive sentiment needs to build up with perhaps a higher guidance or at least 1 upward EPS revision, as no EPS upward revision directly suggests that they are struggling as a business and are losing customers globally. If the next quarter's earnings post a fall in China revenue, AMAT is at risk of losing a third of its revenue which is a huge red flag.

AMAT's Upside Potential

For the other 2 factors regarding pending export license applications and non-linear demand from leading-edge customers, I argue that there is more bullish sentiment from these 2 as AMAT took a conservative stance regarding the export licenses. With Nvidia getting its H20 chip licenses for some customers in China, even if revenue doesn't directly improve next quarter, management can provide an insight into whether the fear is still lingering or whether it has been clarified and is a one-off event. With more and more licenses issued and their guidance noted not to include any licenses getting approved, AMAT's guidance could be simply being conservative like ASML as more and more doubts are getting clarified.

The other factor for a muted outlook was non-linear demand from leading-edge customers. I argue that recent developments, except for the SK Hynix and Samsung in China news have positively impacted AMAT. With higher commitment from TSMC in the US, with the single largest FDI inwards to the US and being exempt from tariffs, TSMC will continue to build more and more fabs as the demand significantly outweighs the supply, noted by Treasury Secretary Bessent that TSMC Arizona meets 7% of US chip demand. So even though their US revenue fell YoY, the development of more and more fabs for TSMC will benefit AMAT as there are more and more developments in 2026 and 2027, which could suggest a rebound in their US revenue. Their Taiwan revenue is also up 60.5% YoY in FY25 Q3, which shows that TSMC is continuing to build out more fabs to meet the high demand as the AI boom is not slowing down.

When it comes to US revenue, a huge doubt and concern before was Intel, with huge struggles and the CEO noting to wait for a major customer before capex in their advanced 14A node. With the recent US government bailout and taking a 10% stake, Intel's new Ohio fab is noted to be 'full steam ahead' by Senator Husted, who helped bring Intel to Ohio. It doesn't matter whether TSMC or Intel is the dominant foundry player. AMAT only cares about and benefits from more and more new fabs getting built, as competition from Intel to TSMC benefits AMAT, as when more semiconductor manufacturing tools are in demand, AMAT's revenue increases.

Despite recent poor performance and more potential poor performance to come, AMAT's balance sheet remains solid. As of the time of writing, they have more cash than debt and the company is not struggling with huge debts or any external struggles, even if the company has a few bad quarters. With operating cash flow increasing YoY and free cash flow relatively flat YoY, AMAT investors shouldn't be extremely worried with their brilliant track record of shareholder returns with buybacks and dividends, as their outstanding shares have reduced by 33% over the last 10 fiscal years. They are struggling with demand as they lose market share, but they are by no means a struggling company with solid shareholder returns and more cash than debt.

Valuation

With recent struggles, AMAT is trading at lower premiums as it is undervalued on a P/E basis compared to the sector median. It is overvalued on a P/S basis compared to the sector median. Compared to historical averages, it depends on P/E non-GAAP, GAAP or P/S and trailing 12 months on a forward basis; some are trading above historical averages and some below, and only by less than 10% at most, so there isn't really any clear over-/undervaluation compared to sector median and historical averages, depending on the metric. However, with many technology firms trading at high P/S, the 4.92 TTM P/S, despite being above the sector median, isn't really a big deal. If P/E trades closer to the sector median, it could suggest at least a 33% upside.

Compared to its Peers:

Compared to AMAT's peers, AMAT seems undervalued on both metrics. However, investors need to remember that AMAT is currently struggling, while some of these manufacturing stocks are soaring such as TSMC. With TSMC's P/E GAAP only being less than 10% different from AMAT's, it could be argued that the other stocks are more overvalued rather than AMAT being undervalued on a P/E basis. However, on a P/S basis, if AMAT does show signs of strength and correct itself towards Lam Research, which is the closest in P/S to AMAT, it could suggest an over 30% upside even if it gets to the midpoint of the current P/S of AMAT and Lam Research.

Conclusion

If AMAT shows signs of strength and bullish sentiment, the upside is huge on a valuation basis, as it is undervalued in most ways one may see it. However, only when they show signs of positive sentiment or that their China struggles may be a short-term issue with the increase in manufacturing tools, I have a neutral view on AMAT for now and further escalations and news are needed for a re-rating.

data.title: Nvidia Remains A Generational Winner
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) remains the queen of AI infrastructure, where it commands the AI board with unmatched compute, networking, and system prowess. In my initial coverage of Nvidia, I crowned Nvidia as the queen on the AI chessboard. My reasoning was that it's the piece that moves in all directions, dominating every corner of AI compute. And for sure, after Q2 FY 2026 earnings, that metaphor holds even truer. The company went ahead to deliver record 2Q revenue worth $46.7Bn which is a 56% increase y/y.

This actually validates the explosive AI demand driving its growth. Specifically, data center sales surged to $41.1Bn. Also, I note that Nvidia's Blackwell platform has started translating into real numbers and is also a leap in investor confidence. And in this regard, I am maintaining my Buy Rating.

Nvidia's management has radiated some confidence. And despite the sky-high expectations, NVDA beat its own guidance and even projected more than 42% revenue growth over the next year. As the most valuable company on earth, this obviously surpassed the ~10% norm for Nasdaq tech companies, as per Bloomberg Intelligence. I believe Nvidia's leadership in AI infrastructure is only the beginning, and Q2's results underscore that the company is on a trajectory of sustained and unprecedented growth.

As of writing this, Nvidia's market cap stands at $4.14Tn. And still at the top as the most valuable company on earth. So, what makes the company warrant a Buy rating even while still at the top?

Well, there are a couple of catalysts and opportunities that I believe will continue pulling the company to a higher market cap.

The Catalysts

Despite the already high growth, I believe Nvidia's true opportunity is still in its early innings. And we are standing at the cusp of what Jensen Huang might call "the big bang of intelligence":

"...We are at the big bang of intelligence, and the United Kingdom’s Goldilocks ecosystem of world-class expertise, outstanding universities and vibrant industries is uniquely positioned to thrive in the age of AI. With AI supercomputers powering state-of-the-art models locally, a new generation of U.K. researchers, developers and entrepreneurs will drive discovery and build the companies of tomorrow..."

Jensen Huang, CEO, Nvidia & UK's Announced Collaboration.

This clearly implies that the demand for AI infrastructure is projected to be astronomical. In fact, during the Q2 call, the CEO sized it in no uncertain terms by saying:

"...Over the next 5 years, we're going to scale into with Blackwell, with Rubin and follow-ons to scale into effectively a $3 trillion to $4 trillion AI infrastructure opportunity...."

Jensen Huang, CEO, 2Q FY 2026 Earnings Call.

And this means that by 2030, annual AI spending could rival and exceed traditional IT spending. Just to provide context, a report by Deloitte estimates $5.2Tn of AI data center capex will be required by 2030 in order to meet compute demand. And this is a multi-trillion-dollar tide lifting NVDA's boat. And with roughly 70% market share in AI compute, Nvidia is well positioned to seize a disproportionate chunk of that spending.

So the question is, what fuels this massive total addressable market, or TAM?

Well, a proliferation of AI use cases and an explosion in model complexity and usage. For instance, in 2023 and 2024, much of the focus was on LLMs. But now, the narrative is shifting to inference (which is actually deploying AI models into everyday services). In very simple terms, inferencing is where real volume will take place. And this is because serving millions of people requires orders of magnitude more compute than the number that was used to train them. Already, NVDA has noted that AI token generation has now increased almost 10x in the last year alone. Companies such as Microsoft (MSFT) went from processing 10Tn tokens a month to 100Tn in its Azure cloud within a year. And remember, each token processed is basically work that is done by GPUs.

And what's even more interesting is agentic AI, which doesn't just respond to one prompt only but can plan, take actions, and perform multi-step tasks. And this is why I believe that as AI evolves to be more interactive and autonomous, then the demand for inference compute will also be increasing beyond the current projected growth rates. And for sure, this is one of the most underappreciated drivers for Nvidia. The way I see it is that we are not just going to have AI users, but rather will have AI doing more tasks per user and consuming far more GPU cycles. Again, I want you to remember that every time an AI agent plans a complex task, it translates to thousands of GPU operations where previously there might have been only one.

"...demand only grows. One shot chatbots have evolved into reasoning agentic AI that research, plan and use tools, driving orders of magnitude jump in compute for both training and inference. Agentic AI is reaching maturity and has opened the enterprise market to build domain and company-specific AI agents for enterprise workflows, products and services..."

Jensen Huang, CEO, 2Q FY 2026 Earnings Call.

So now that the narrative is shifting to inferencing, Nvidia is positioning itself to capture this wave. For instance, it recently announced Rubin CPX. Which is a new class of inference-focused GPUs, alongside its next-gen Rubin architecture. And when I look at the commercials, I note that Nvidia's $100Mn of investment in a Rubin AI system could yield $5Bn in annual revenue for an AI service provider. And this is why I believe this 50X ratio shows how valuable AI compute is.

"...We expect annual AI infrastructure investments to continue growing, driven by the several factors: reasoning agentic AI requiring orders of magnitude more training and inference compute, global build- outs for sovereign AI, enterprise AI adoption, and the arrival of physical AI and robotics..."

Colette Kress, CFO, 2Q FY 2026 Earnings Call.

Beyond cloud and enterprise, there are definitely new verticals emerging for AI often. I will not dwell on all of them, but just to mention some of them, such as physical AI. Another instance is sovereign AI, which is nations and governmental organizations building their AI supercomputers for national use. As an example, just recently UK's collaboration with Nvidia to build an AI infrastructure featuring more than 120K Blackwell GPUs.

In summary, I would say that the catalyst is the AI revolution itself. And it's fast accelerating. And Nvidia is literally at the heart of the revolution. It's not just enjoying a one-time boom. But instead, it's creating new markets with its innovations.

Valuation

In terms of valuation, I'd argue that the valuation remains reasonable given the growth and margin. I note that NVDA scores an F valuation grade.

However, what catches my attention is the PEG, which validates my thesis. The company has an FWD non-GAAP PEG of 1.06x, while the sector median stands at 1.86x. This suggests that Nvidia is 43% discounted relative to the sector median. And this clearly convinces me that the company's valuation is not outlandish. The company is expected to grow by 45% to 60% annually in the next few years, while paying ~40x earnings for that kind of growth is in line with growth companies historically.

I would say that Nvidia's valuation reflects excellence. And by traditional measures, it is quite expensive. However, those measures don't fully account for the unprecedented growth and profitability combination that Nvidia has.

Nvidia's Key Risk

At a $4.14Tn market cap, there is definitely a valuation risk. As of now, Nvidia's stock is priced for high growth, and this means that any hiccup, whether it's a growth slowdown or broader market rotation, could cause volatility. At FWD non-GAAP P/E of 37x, it means expectations are quite high. Nevertheless, if NVDA continues with a brilliant execution, then definitely, the earnings will justify the price.

Final Thoughts

Usually in chess, when you have a dominant position and control the board, the wise move is often to press the advantage. And that's exactly what Nvidia is doing. It's pressing its advantage in AI infrastructure. And despite being the most valuable company at $4.14Tn market cap, there is still so much more that NVDA has to unpack and lead the AI revolution from the front.

data.title: Breaking Down The Bearish Narrative
data.description:
The bears have been beaten down the past three years, but they have not yet hibernated. The tariff-induced correction during the first quarter of this year fueled fears of recession and an end to the bull market, which breathed new life into their narrative. Yet the post-Liberation Day rally, which is now in its sixth month, has been one for the record books, with the S&P 500 surging more than 30% to multiple new all-time highs. I was concerned this summer that the president’s tariffs would revive inflation and slow growth to such a degree that the economy would face a period of stagflation. To some degree, that occurred. The rate of inflation has ticked up three of the past four months, and growth slowed dramatically during the first half of this year, with job creation stagnant the past four months. I feared that might open the door to a pullback at best or another correction at worst, but neither came to pass. Still, there were no indications that the economic expansion was at risk, and this is the bedrock of the bull market.

The underlying premise of the bearish narrative is that valuations for the broad market are excessive. I can’t deny that fact. The cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings [CAPE] ratio for the S&P 500 is currently at 37.5, which makes this the third most expensive market on record.

The problem is that valuations are a horrible timing tool. John Maynard Keynes told us that “the market can remain irrational longer than you can remain solvent,” assuming you are betting against the market. In other words, the market can stay expensive for a very long time, defying what we perceive to be fair value, due to a multitude of factors. Once valuations are excessive, you need to see negative rates of change in the vital signs for the economy to such an extent that the expansion is in peril. That usually requires excesses in the economy that need to be extinguished. I can’t identify any excesses today, but I think the most likely one in the future will be related to the buildout of AI. Valuations on their own are not a good reason to avoid the stock market, and minimal exposure will likely lead to very subpar returns. Where valuations do become relevant is in predicting forward returns.

The forward returns for the market show losses across all time frames when the CAPE multiple is above 35, as it is today. Obviously, the returns improve dramatically when we start from lower and lower valuations. This is an ominous statistic for buy-and-hold investors who passively invest new money in an index today. I have a lot more confidence in these numbers over a 3-10 year time period than I do the next 12 months. That is because another statistic I shared last week shows that when the Fed has cut rates with the S&P 500 within 2% of an all-time high, the index has been higher every single time one year later. Remember that these forward returns are averages.

This data is consistent with the chart below, which I first shared with investors two years ago. It suggests that we may be in the early innings of a secular bear market, as we were from 2000 through 2010. The culprits this time would probably be higher rates of inflation and rising long-term interest rates. This isn’t as bearish as it looks. Within a secular bear market, there can be multiple cyclical bull markets. During the last secular bear, there was a very profitable bull run from 2003 through 2007. That is a long time to sit out of the market if you are bearish.

The best strategy to employ if we are in a secular bear market is a tactical one. That means be selective about the securities you own, maintain cash in portfolios to be opportunistic, stay diversified across asset classes, and maintain international exposure as well. There is always a bull market somewhere, and it is a tactical portfolio's mission to find it.

data.title: AstraZeneca: Consistent Growth And Upcoming Catalysts Make This A Buy
data.description:
The Cambridge, UK-based AstraZeneca PLC (AZN) was founded in 1999 as an amalgamation of the pharmaceutical companies Zeneca Group, based in Britain, and Astra AB, from Sweden. The company’s primary listing is in the UK (LSE: AZN), but it is also listed on the Nasdaq (through ADR shares) and Stockholm exchanges. It has six main R&D centers, in Cambridge, UK, Gothenburg, Sweden, Gaithersburg, MD, Boston, MA, and two in China, Beijing and Shanghai.

AstraZeneca’s main focus, historically, has been on oncology, antiviral therapies, and inflammatory diseases, but in 2013 after a major corporate restructure, the company consolidated its focus into three key areas: 1) respiratory inflammation and autoimmunity; 2) cardiovascular and metabolic diseases; and 3) oncology. They also moved into rare diseases with their buyout of Alexion Pharmaceuticals in 2021.

Based on 2024 revenues (USD), AstraZeneca was ranked #5 globally among all pharmaceutical/biotech companies, behind Merck (MRK), Pfizer (PFE), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and Abbvie (ABBV), and #4 in terms of current market cap, which stands at $236 billion. In the most recent reporting period (1H 2025), the company generated 43% of its revenue in the US, with Europe at 21%.

In terms of treatment areas, the company derived the largest share of its revenue (43%) from its oncology division, which also enjoyed the strongest growth (21% on a TTM basis). Its 6 main revenue-generating drugs all enjoyed revenue growth year-on-year, which shows it is at the forefront of this treatment area. Tagrisso, its best-selling treatment for small-cell lung cancer, generated $3.49 billion in revenue and enjoyed 10% growth globally. Of the global list of top-selling drugs in 2024, it was ranked #22. Only AstraZeneca’s SGLT2 inhibitor (Farxiga) for the treatment of type 2 diabetes, chronic (long-term) heart failure and chronic kidney disease, ranked #20 on the 2024 list, earned more. It generated $4.21 billion in revenue in H1 2025, showing an even more impressive 13% growth.

Also worth mentioning in its oncology segment is Imfinzi, its immune checkpoint inhibitor for the treatment of lung and various other cancers ($2.72 billion in 1H 2025, at 21% growth), its PARP inhibitor Lynparza ($1.56 billion in H1 2025 revenue at 9% growth), and Calquence, approved to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia ($1.26 billion revenue in 1H 2025, also at 9% growth). Most exciting in their current offering is Enhurtu for the treatment of breast cancer, which enjoyed a whopping 38% sales growth year over year.

Enhurtu takes a novel approach to treating breast cancer, the most common and deadliest form of cancer for women. The drug targets the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), which is present in a large number of breast cancer diagnoses and poses a significant risk if patients develop brain metastases, which happens in about a third of cases. Enhurtu showed very promising data in recent trials, with survival rates that handily beat out its chief rival Kadcyla, made by Genentech, part of Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY). In my estimation, Enhurtu is set to see sustained revenue growth going forward – GlobalData estimates $11.2 billion in annual sales by 2030 (representing a CAGR of over 20% from 2024 sales figures) – but oncology is an incredibly competitive space and rival companies are progressing treatments through trials at a breathtaking pace. Encouragingly, Enhurtu has been approved for other indications including gastric, non small-cell lung cancer and any HER2-positive solid tumors. The company is continuing trials to increase the number of indications for this blockbuster drug that, in time, could overtake even Farxiga and Tagrisso in annual sales.

In its respiratory and immunology division, Symbicort (1.44 billion 1H 2025 revenue) remained the largest contributor, but saw a 2% revenue decrease year over year. Ultomiris, for the treatment of several rare autoimmune and blood-related conditions, remains the largest revenue driver in its rare diseases portfolio, enjoying 24% revenue growth and $2.23 billion in 1H 2025. In total, AstraZeneca generated revenue of at least $170 million each in 17 different products. Their diversification significantly de-risks their portfolio, especially as drugs reach a patent cliff and generics steal market share. Their strong pipeline seems more than likely to offset lost sales due to generic competition. However, their diversification also means that the potential impact of a single product may be limited, even if it outperforms expectations. In all, the company seems to be firing on all cylinders, and this is reflected in their sustained revenue growth over the last few years. The enormous revenue growth generated from Tagrisso alongside its Covid vaccine Vaxzevria (generating $3.9 billion) in 2021 has slowed but, with its new offerings, growth has returned somewhat.

The company’s operating expenses have increased over time, but, encouragingly, most of this increase has come from R&D. As seen in the chart below, the proportion of their revenue being directed to R&D has increased, while the opposite is the case for the S,G&A expenses. This is a positive, showing increasing efficiencies in their business and a strong and sustained focus on long-term growth.

The company’s gross margin has remained consistent in the 81-83% range over recent years (currently 82.4%), while its net margin has improved – on a TTM basis – to 14.7%, its highest since 2017. Also, its EPS has ticked up nicely, showing almost 29% growth on a TTM basis.

With these metrics in hand, we would expect to see a steady appreciation in its share price, and this is certainly the case. Its 10-year chart (below) is a model of consistent appreciation. If we add in the dividend, the stock has, roughly speaking, kept pace with the general market if not slightly outperformed it.

What investors want to know is, what is the likelihood of this more recent trend continuing? Do shares represent good value with a positive risk-reward ratio at their current price? To answer these questions, we’ll delve into their pipeline, potential catalysts over the next year or so, and evaluate the company’s financials and valuation in more detail. There is also some recent news to discuss.

Pipeline and Catalysts

On September 17, the company brought out two press releases. The first was negative: they were discontinuing the trial of Fasenra for COPD after it failed to meet the primary endpoint. The drug is already approved for the treatment of asthma in more than 80 countries, and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis in adults in more than 60 countries. While this is disappointing news, it is not game-changing for the overall thesis of the company or even the drug itself. Sales of Fasenra for the approved indications mentioned above represented just 3.3% of first half 2025 total revenue for the company. Moreover, this trial was just 1 of 12 ongoing studies that AstraZeneca was expecting results from in 2H 2025.

Also released the same day was news that Saphnelo "demonstrated a statistically significant and clinically meaningful reduction in disease activity compared to placebo" for treating systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) through subcutaneous injection. The drug was proven both safe and efficacious. This is obviously positive news as the disease affects over 3.4 million people globally. Saphnelo generated $304 million last year, through its IV-based treatment in moderate to severe SLE, and saw a 49% revenue growth. The benefits of subcutaneous treatment in general – as outlined here and here – are lower infection risk, simplified administration (at home and by the patient themselves), and therefore improved convenience and some cost savings.

While the company’s drug pipeline is extensive and holds several promising treatments, prospective investors can never be sure what treatments will truly move the needle, as this is an incredibly dynamic space and there are so many other factors to consider, not least of all what other companies are doing behind the scenes to target the same indications. So, for pharmaceutical companies that offer such a large array of products, what I tend to do is take a step back from the current or prospective sales figures for individual products and instead focus on how the company is managing itself, specifically: investments, cost savings, management effectiveness, and track record. Some of these might be considered subjective, qualitative measures, but they are important.

Their pipeline is their main focus for investment and clearly extensive; the company is anticipating several catalysts in the form of trial data and approvals being sought in various locales globally. Their track record here is excellent. In the six months prior to reporting 1H 2025 results, they saw 19 approvals in key regions (U.S., EU, China and Japan) and have plans to deliver at least 20 NMEs (new molecular entities) by 2030 and $80 billion in total revenue. From their figure of $54 billion in 2024 revenue, this would represent a CAGR of about 6.8%. On top of this, they aim to continue their track record of reducing S,G&A spend relative to revenue, thereby anticipating an operating margin around the mid-30s (currently 25.4%).

Doing so would allow the company to divert more of its revenue toward both R&D and M&A, driving further revenue growth into the future. The company is a serial acquirer, having purchased at least 20 smaller companies since its founding—the most recent was EsoBiotec in May 2025. Generally speaking, management has proved itself adept in its acquisitions, which should give shareholders confidence going forward.

While a 6.8% forward revenue growth rate does not necessarily scream at us, their efforts to improve operating efficiencies in combination with their strong product portfolio and rich pipeline commands attention, and any revenue accretion through M&A activity should enhance this figure still further. EPS should continue to grow and, in combination with a healthy dividend (current yield 2% based on a 70% payout ratio and near 16% FCF margin), shareholders should be richly rewarded. Let’s take a deeper look at its financials and present valuation.

Financials and Valuation

AstraZeneca has cash and equivalents of about $7.11 billion, and total debt of $32.9 billion (just over $2 billion in short-term debt). It has total assets valued at $112 billion, which includes $21 billion in goodwill on the balance sheet. Total liabilities come to $67.6 billion, giving an assets/liabilities ratio of 1.65. Even stripping away the goodwill, the figure is 1.34.

The company has a lower quick and current ratio than I tend to find comfortable as an investor, but because it generates a lot of FCF and can fairly easily pay off its debt I am less concerned. Low numbers here would be most worrisome in pre-profit companies or those spending aggressively on growth in an unproven market, but this is not AstraZeneca. Their strong business model and FCF generation ($11.2 billion in the last twelve months) mitigates a lot of the leverage risk here. Its debt/equity ratio is healthy for a company of its size, and with interest rates falling globally, this should be less of a burden as interest expenses decline slightly.

In terms of its investments and uses of cash, the company has become increasingly efficient, seen in its rapidly improving returns since 2021 (below). This is an excellent sign.

In terms of valuation, the company trades at a forward P/E of 21.4 on a non-GAAP basis. This is 13.5% below the sector median and 31% below its historical average of nearly 31. Based on consensus estimates or earnings growth, it has a current (GAAP) PEG ratio of 1.

Seeking Alpha currently give it a C- grade for valuation, but it is clear from its P/S and Fwd P/E ratios that the company has become cheaper (in terms of its earnings multiple) over time, despite its steadily improving share price. This is also reflected in its increasing earnings and FCF yields since 2020.

Taken together, I would consider the company fairly priced, if not slightly undervalued, given its sustained growth, track record of innovative treatment, and solid pipeline of new products and new indications going forward.

In terms of what price I’d be tempted to start a position here, I’d look at its technicals, which might be instructive here. In its 5-year chart, the stock is clearly in a broad uptrend, about halfway between its upper and lower channels at present. I would be tempted to start a position if the share price fell a bit further (below $70), but only because I believe the company will sustain its consistent growth over the next few years and may possibly bounce off that trend line soon. I’m be willing to be patient with this one, knowing full well that I may miss the boat. That is the risk you take as a bargain stock hunter.

Conclusion

I believe AstraZeneca to be a well-managed, solid growth compounder. It has a superb track record, a wide moat in the industry, especially oncology, and has improved its operating margins through careful investments and business growth. I see no reason why management can’t sustain the wonderful progress they have made, and with their strong pipeline and numerous catalysts for the rest of 2025 and into 2026, I anticipate decent price appreciation. Will it beat the market over the net five or ten years? I’d probably give that a 50/50 chance, but with a possible recession looming alongside a stagflationary situation in the general economy, investors might rotate into these types of stocks—that is, those non-cyclical companies with strong balance sheets, decent dividends, and a track record of winning through difficult periods in the past.

I would urge investors to keep an eye on developments in China, given some regulatory concerns and recent investigations by the Chinese authorities at the end of last year. Some of these are still ongoing, and should be looked into by current and prospective shareholders. Revenue from China represented 12% of its total in 2024.

For shareholders looking for a long-term lower-risk equity, I would give this a Buy rating. If the NYSE ADR shares drop meaningfully below $70, I’d make it a strong buy.
