data.title: Seix High Yield Bond SMA Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The second quarter of 2025 was marked by a dramatic reversal in sentiment, driven by shifting expectations around trade policy, inflation, and monetary policy. After a turbulent start to the quarter triggered by the April 2 “Liberation Day” announcements, markets stabilized and rallied into quarter-end, buoyed by easing inflation data, resilient corporate fundamentals, and renewed investor appetite for risk.

BB and single-B rated bonds performed largely in line with each other during the second quarter. The ICE BofA BB U.S. High Yield Index (H0A1) increased by 3.46%, while the ICE BofA Single-B U.S. High Yield Index (H0A2) increased by 3.47%. CCCs outperformed in 2Q25, reversing what we saw during the first quarter as the ICE BofA CCC & Lower U.S. High Yield Index (H0A3) increased by 4.28%. However, for the year-to-date period, BBs outperformed single-Bs and CCCs. The H0A1 index increased by 4.96% for the first half of the year, the H0A2 index increased by 4.20%, and the H0A3 index increased by 3.58%. BBBs, as measured by the ICE BofA BBB U.S. Corporate Index (C0A4), increased by 4.29% for the first half of the year.

The option-adjusted spread for the ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index (H0A0) began the year at 292 basis points (bps), widened to 461 bps on April 7 shortly after the Liberation Day tariff announcements, and ended the quarter at 296 bps—almost back to where we began the year.

Performance

The Seix High Yield Bond SMA that we manage increased by 2.62% (gross) in 2Q25 (1.99% net). This performance was 95 bps fewer than the benchmark, the ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Cash Pay Index (J0A0), which increased by 3.57%. The top contributing sectors to relative performance included cable satellite, gaming & leisure, and restaurants/food/beverage/supermarkets. The bottom contributing sectors to relative performance included healthcare/pharmaceuticals, retailers, and transportation/ automotive/aerospace.

The portfolio increased by 4.20% (gross) for the year-to-date period (2.91% net). This performance was 37 bps fewer than the benchmark, ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Cash Pay Index (J0A0), which increased by 4.57%. The top contributing sectors to relative performance included cable satellite, chemicals, and gaming & leisure. The bottom contributing sectors to relative performance included healthcare/pharmaceuticals, telecommunications, and hotels & REITs.

Our longer-term objective is to provide a smoother ride with outperformance through the full credit cycle and the strongest relative performance generated during periods of dislocation when the market is offering a plethora of attractively-priced securities.

Current Strategy & Outlook

As we enter the second half of 2025, our strategy remains grounded in disciplined credit analysis and a focus on the higher-quality segments of the high yield market. The macroeconomic backdrop continues to be defined by elevated policy uncertainty, slowing growth tempered by economic resilience, and sticky inflation. These conditions, combined with valuations that are at the tighter end of their historical range, reinforce our up-in-quality bias. This is further supported by the relative strength of BB-rated issuers, which have demonstrated more stable earnings and greater financial flexibility than CCC-rated issuers, which remain under pressure, with compressed margins and more constrained access to the capital markets. As a result, we remain underweight CCCs.

To obtain the top/bottom holdings calculation methodology, call 800-243-4361.

Seix Investment Advisors is a division of Virtus Fixed Income Advisers, LLC (“VFIA”), an SEC registered investment adviser.

While fundamentals have begun to show signs of modest erosion, we believe the overall credit quality of the high yield market remains strong. The recent surge in refinancing activity, combined with ample liquidity and muted near-term maturity walls, has helped to push out maturities, which can be helpful in lowering default risk. Leverage for high yield issuers rose to 4.08x in 1Q, though this is still below the long-term average of 4.29x. Interest coverage declined modestly to 4.70x but remains well above the historical median, suggesting high yield issuers have above-average levels of financial flexibility. The increase in leverage and decline in interest coverage was due in part to declining profit margins, which have fallen to a four-year low. These trends reflect a market that is fundamentally sound but increasingly vulnerable to macro headwinds, particularly in lower-rated cohorts.

From a rating perspective, the trends have been less favorable this year. The year-to-date upgrade to downgrade ratio is at 0.88x to 1 (suggesting more downgrades than upgrades this year) compared to 1.22x for the same period in 2024. The upgrade to downgrade ratio was 0.54x in June, which was the second-worst month of the year (April being the worst). Looking beneath the surface, the year- to-date upgrade to downgrade ratios for BBs, single-Bs, and CCCs reveal that higher-quality assets show positive ratings momentum while lower-quality ones trend negatively. This pattern has remained consistent for at least the past year. Retail and consumer products experienced the greatest number of downgrades year-to-date.

From a valuation perspective, spreads remain tight by historical standards, but yields continue to offer attractive entry points, particularly in BBs and select single-Bs. Yields began the year just inside long-term median levels at 7.47%, widened to 8.65% (75th percentile), and ended the quarter at 7.05% (32nd percentile). The relatively attractive yields combined with solid fundamentals has supported trading levels for the higher-quality part of the market.

We remain constructive on high yield, but are maintaining a cautious and selective approach, favoring quality, liquidity, and flexibility as we navigate the remainder of the year. We believe this environment favors active management and security selection, with a focus on issuers that offer compelling risk-adjusted income and downside protection.

data.title: Crescent Capital: Time To Moderate My Expectations
data.description:
Crescent Capital BDC, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:CCAP) shareholders, including myself, faced CCAP's negative stock price performance in 2025. Since the beginning of the year, its stock price has dropped by over 18%. Luckily for us, that negative performance is somewhat offset by CCAP's attractive dividends.

Still, with decreasing interest rates (the Fed has just provided us with another 25 bps cut), many investors are left wondering what they can expect from CCAP and whether their dividends are sustainable. It's not surprising at all, especially given a BDC so susceptible to interest rate cuts. This a follow-up coverage to my previous article about CCAP, which I decided to prepare in light of new financial data available (indicating increasing problems with non-accruals) and the Fed's most recent decision. Let's review this.

CCAP's overview and its susceptibility to interest rate changes

CCAP is a debt-oriented BDC with high exposure to first lien debt, amounting to 91% of its portfolio. Having invested in nearly 190 companies (187 to be exact), CCAP's investments amount to $1.6B on a fair value basis.

I've mentioned above that CCAP is highly susceptible to interest rate cuts. It looks that way for CCAP, because 97% of its debt investments are structured with a floating rate. As a result of that, each interest rate cut has a negative impact on CCAP's investment income. To some degree, that's also the case for CCAP's own debt financing, but nearly 50% of its outstanding debt has a fixed-rate structure, so it's not like a reduced cost of debt is sufficient to offset reduced income potential.

Since the Fed started cutting interest rates around a year ago, we can clearly see the effects of that. Just look at the chart below and focus on CCAP's weighted average yield. It decreased from 12.2% in Q2 2024 to 10.4% in Q1 2025. It remained at the same level in Q2 2025 (as we didn't have any interest rate cuts in this period), but now - I'm afraid the weighted average yield will decline even further. Since we're already near the end of Q3 2025, the effects of the most recent interest rate cut will be truly visible in the data for Q4 2025.

These changes naturally translated into CCAP's ability to generate net investment income. When you look at its NII per share for the last couple of quarters, you'll easily notice that it dropped by ~22% in Q2 2025 when compared to Q2 2024. The regular dividend coverage, once showcasing a great 140% level, currently stands at ~10% and is set to decrease in the near term due to the last (and potentially upcoming) interest rate changes.

However, we can't look at interest rate changes only from the perspective of quantifiable metrics. There's one great benefit interest rate cuts that all BDCs share, regardless of their floating-rate debt exposure—improving the credit quality of their portfolio companies.

Just think about it. The Fed started increasing interest rates in mid-2022. It's been three years since then, and that's quite a lot of time for businesses to operate under a relatively high-interest rate environment (especially with such dynamic increases following a long period of low-interest rates). Many BDC management teams mentioned that businesses operating on the lower end of the liquidity profile were struggling with that.

The case isn't any different for CCAP, and we can easily see how that manifests in its portfolio, especially when reviewing Q1-Q2 2025 data. Look at the chart below presenting CCAP's internal performance ratings.

We can see two crucial changes:

  1. The share of 3-rated investments (performing below expectations) increased in Q4 2024–Q2 2025 when compared to Q2–Q3 2024.
  2. The share of 4/5-rated investments (performing materially/substantially below expectations) increased in Q2 2025 to 3%.

These are concerning changes, and they represent how much some businesses are struggling. CCAP's non-accruals have increased substantially to ~2.4% at fair value in Q2 2025 vs. 0.9% in Q4 2024. Still, it's crucial to remember that internal performance ratings serve as a monitoring list for the management, so it could stay proactive when it comes to managing any potential defaults.

As the management commented during the last Earnings Call:

It is worth noting that as of quarter-end, as a percentage of total investments at fair value, CCAP's watch list, which we define as 3, 4, and 5 rated investments, was 14%, whereas our nonaccruals at fair value were 2.4%, a nearly 12% gap. This is in contrast to an analysis of our public peers where this gap is approximately 6%. We believe this reflects our philosophy and our culture of being forward-looking in terms of maintaining our watch list. We do not, for example, wait until there is a default for moving something down the risk rating scale.

We have to know what to expect from CCAP

Last quarter, CCAP distributed $0.47 per share to its shareholders, which consisted of a regular dividend amounting to $0.42 per share and a special distribution amounting to $0.05 per share. As BDC investors, we care about income a lot. While the management claims that CCAP's regular dividend coverage is in excess of 10%, I wouldn't be as optimistic.

Excluding $0.02 per share of one-time accelerated amortization related to deferred financing costs, NII was $0.48 per share. Importantly, earnings remain in excess of our dividend with 110% base dividend coverage for the quarter.

Let me state right from the get-go - I don't think CCAP will be able to pay supplemental or special dividends for too long. Its total dividend coverage already nearly matches its NII (even after the one-time adjustment mentioned by the management), and the most recent interest rate cut has yet to have its effect on CCAP's income potential.

With just regular dividends taken into account, CCAP's coverage is likely to fall below 110%, the management mentioned by the end of this year. If the Fed continues its cutting journey, CCAP's coverage could even fall short of 100%.

Still, even when assuming that CCAP will only be able to pay regular dividends in the upcoming quarters, that's still quite an attractive yield of 10.7%. On top of that, CCAP trades at a ~20% discount to its NAV, so it's not like we're targeting an overvalued BDC.

Regardless, even though I've built a certain stake of shares in CCAP, I'm not eager to buy anymore. I respect its portfolio composition in terms of asset mix and its preventive approach to default management, but I haven't expected such material increases in non-accruals. When combined with the risk of losing the ability to maintain the current level of dividend payments, I'll be more comfortable with holding onto the stake I have and reinvesting dividends elsewhere.

Therefore, CCAP is a hold for me.
